text: The surgeon performs a reanastomosis of the small bowel, with the umbilical artery visible nearby. A large incision is made towards the umbilical artery. The uterus is observed to be intact, with no remnants of the fallopian tube visible. It appears that a previous dissection was performed on a nearby vessel.

The surgeon identifies fibrotic tissue and carefully incises parallel to the umbilical artery, ensuring the uterus is not affected. The obturatory vein is visualized, and the incision is made just past it, directly contacting the endopedic fascia.
The surgeon removes adipose tissue, revealing the sacrosiatic ligament and sciatic spine. The vein is visible centrally in the surgical field, with the sacrosanct ligament beginning at this level.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks, including the arcuate tendinitis of the levator ani muscle and the coccygeus muscle covering the sacrosanct ligament. The endopelvic fascia is opened to access the sciatic spine and the alcock canal, with the sciatic spine grasped for further manipulation.

The surgeon coagulates the ligament while carefully pushing the tissue behind to protect the nerves. The thin sacrostatic ligament is visible, with vessels running near the static nerve. The surgeon stays close to the wall to avoid missing the entrance to the alcock canal. Muscle fibers from the coccygeal muscle are observed.

The surgeon examines the strongest part of the sacrosanct ligament, noting its firmness and potential for compression. The tissue appears blocked, and a vein runs behind the ligament, requiring careful navigation. An anatomical variation is observed, possibly indicating a duplication of the sacrosanct ligament.

The surgeon makes an incision just under the side of the aesthetic plane to open the alcove canal, ensuring there is no blockage of the rectal branch passage to the ligament. The incision reveals a deeper and wider section of the alcove canal. The camera is adjusted to detach some adhesions.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying the mesentery of the bowel and the site of bowel anastomosis. The structures are visually assessed to determine the orientation of the anti-reflux valve relative to the bladder.

The surgeon assesses the implantation of the Monti, noting it is positioned too much on the dome and under tension. The stoma appears visually clear without stenosis. The surgeon plans to cut the bowel at a specific level, retrieve a piece of bowel, construct an anti-reflexing valve in the bladder, and reanastomose the two bowel segments.

The surgeon identifies the distant reanastomosis site and carefully suspends the bowel segment without devascularizing it. They prepare to excise approximately two centimeters of bowel in onion ring sections, ensuring the presence of a vessel in the mesentery to avoid stenosis.

The surgeon observes a visible pellicle near the epigastric region and considers taking tissue on the side of a vessel. The surgeon attempts to pass through the mesentery, evaluating the best approach for the procedure.

The surgeon makes the first incision using a monopolar instrument to minimize bleeding risk. A second incision is made to assess bleeding, creating a long flap intended for placement inside the bladder as part of the anti-reflux valve. A small piece of bowel is preserved for anastomosis with the previously constructed Monti, ensuring it remains well vascularized.
The surgeon places two stitches to stabilize the raw area of the bowel anastomosis, preparing the site for further stabilization.

The surgeon reinforces the stitch to prevent tension on the first staples, ensuring they do not release and cause the anastomosis to open. Care is taken not to tighten too much at the base of the flap to avoid strangulation. The presence of a bitters on the stitch and the catheter balloon at this level is noted, indicating where the surgeon plans to make a cut.

The surgeon attempts a longitudinal incision inside the bladder, aiming to cut the muscle layer without involving the mucosa. The incision reveals the bladder mucosa, indicating a need for adjustment.
The surgeon begins the reanastomosis between the pneumontic flap and the bladder, starting with the distal suture. The tube is carefully closed, with adjustments made to reduce the size of the hole.

The surgeon closes the small mucosal holes in the bladder, repositioning one hole to a lower location. The distal part of the tube is positioned downward, and the surgeon prepares to close the tube longitudinally.

The surgeon observes bleeding, indicating good vascularization, and begins constructing the anti-reflux system by placing the tube inside a double valve created with the bladder. The tube's trajectory is submucosal, following a longitudinal incision. The anti-reflux valve is completed, and the surgeon releases the stiff front panel to prevent tension on the stoma.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder to reduce tension at the anastomosis site between the old and new mantis. A Vicryl-IIO running suture is used to complete the anastomosis between these structures.

The surgeon fixes the anterior part of the bladder with a suture to the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring proper alignment for catheterization. The intestinal anastomosis is attached to the abdominal wall to reduce tension in the mesentery of the Monti. The valve appears to be continuous.
